mappings.rationale: Zalasiewicz covers the geologic time scale including Precambrian, Paleozoic, Mesozoic, and Cenozoic eras and their defining characteristics.
Benton covers the succession of life through each geological era and period, providing the biological context for each time division described in this section.
Thomson covers the fossils characteristic of each geological period, relevant to understanding the biological markers that define the eras and periods in this section.
Norman covers the Mesozoic Era's dominant land animals, directly relevant to the Triassic, Jurassic, and Cretaceous periods described in this section.
Wignall covers the mass extinction events that mark the boundaries between geological periods, including the Permian-Triassic and Cretaceous-Paleogene events.
Redfern provides an overview of Earth history through the geological eras, including the major environmental and biological changes in each period.
Woodward covers the Quaternary Period and the Pleistocene Epoch in detail, including glaciations and climate oscillations described in this section.
Evans covers glacial periods throughout Earth history, directly relevant to the Pleistocene Epoch and earlier glaciations in the Paleozoic and Precambrian.
Molnar discusses the tectonic configuration of continents through geological time, providing the paleogeographic context for each era and period.
Covers the rock formations characteristic of each geological era, from Precambrian basement rocks to Cenozoic sediments.
The Charlesworths cover the evolutionary events that define each geological period, from the Cambrian explosion to the rise of mammals in the Cenozoic.
Lenton covers Earth system evolution through geological time, including the major transitions that mark era and period boundaries.
Wood covers human evolution during the Quaternary Period and Pleistocene Epoch, directly relevant to this section's treatment of the latest geological time.
Maslin discusses climate variations through geological time, relevant to understanding the climatic conditions that characterized each era and period.
Catling covers the origin and early evolution of life in the Precambrian, directly relevant to this section's treatment of Precambrian time.
Ellam covers the radiometric dating methods used to establish the absolute ages of era and period boundaries in the geological time scale.
Tuniz covers radioactive decay and dating, essential to establishing the numerical ages (540 Ma, 245 Ma, 66.4 Ma) that define the era boundaries.
Gosden covers the Quaternary Period from a human perspective, relevant to this section's treatment of the Pleistocene Epoch and its connection to human prehistory.
